title: Laghu Atiyāra — The Short Confession of the Twelve Lay Vows' Transgressions
summary: This is the layperson's short examination of conscience. After first reviewing right faith (samyaktva), the reciter walks through the twelve vows of a Jain householder one by one, names the five recognised 'slips' (atichāra) that can weaken each vow, and asks pardon for any committed that day (or that night). It is recited every day inside the Devasi (evening) and Rāi (dawn) pratikramaṇa as the fourth of the six daily obligations, and it closes each vow with the formula 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' — 'may my wrongdoing be in vain / undone.'
ritualPlacement: Laghu Atiyāra fills the fourth Āvaśyaka (Paḍikkamaṇa — reflection and atonement) of the daily ritual. In the Devasi flow it follows the Dvādaśāvarta Vāndaṇā (guru-veneration) and precedes the Muni Vandanā and the sajjhāy cluster. It is the SHORT atichāra, used only in the two daily pratikramaṇas, Devasi and Rāi (the bracketed cue in the text — 'if it is night, say rātri-sambandhī' — is the substitution that adapts it from the evening to the dawn observance). The fortnightly, seasonal and annual pratikramaṇas (Pakkhī, Cāumāsī, Sāṁvatsarī) replace it with the EXTENDED atichāra, `bruhad-atiyar`; the present short text is therefore not carried into those longer observances.
authorAndSource: The text is a traditional vidhi-formula, not the signed work of a single named author; it is reproduced here verbatim from the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha Gaccha) edition of *Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit*. Its doctrinal frame — the twelve śrāvaka vows with five atichāras each, prefaced by the five atichāras of samyaktva — derives from the canonical and post-canonical śrāvakācāra tradition (notably the Upāsakadaśā-sūtra and the Tattvārtha-sūtra ch. 7). The language is mixed: Ardhamāgadhī/Prākrit atichāra-lists embedded in a classical-Gujarati procedural matrix, with occasional Sanskrit phrasing.
structureArc: The confession opens with a samyaktva (right-faith) preamble (the block before vow 1): it affirms the Arhat as god, true sādhus as guru, and Jina-taught dharma, then scrutinises faith's five atichāras — śaṅkā, kāṅkṣā, vicikitsā, para-pāṣaṇḍī-praśaṁsā, para-pāṣaṇḍī-saṁstava. Verses 1–5 then take the five aṇuvrata (lesser vows): 1 non-violence (prāṇātipāta-viramaṇa), 2 truthfulness (mṛṣāvāda-viramaṇa), 3 non-stealing (adattādāna-viramaṇa), 4 chastity/contentment (śīla / svadāra-santoṣa), 5 limiting possessions (parigraha-parimāṇa). Verses 6–8 cover the three guṇavrata: 6 limiting directions (diś-vrata), 7 limiting consumption (bhogopabhoga — which additionally enumerates the fifteen karmādāna trades), 8 avoiding purposeless harm (anartha-daṇḍa-viramaṇa). Verses 9–12 cover the four śikṣāvrata: 9 equanimity-sitting (sāmāyika), 10 daily contraction of limits (deśāvagāsika), 11 ascetic fast-day (pauṣadha), 12 sharing with worthy guests (atithi-saṁvibhāga). Each section ends with its five atichāras and the day/night confession-refrain.
recurringImagery: There is no metaphorical imagery here; the unifying motif is instead a fixed liturgical TEMPLATE repeated for every vow, and understanding it once unlocks the whole text. Each unit runs: (a) announce the vow by number and name; (b) state its scope or sub-divisions (e.g. 'nine-fold' for possessions, 'three-fold' for directions, 'four-fold' for pauṣadha) and the personal rule (niyama) and the leeway (jayaṇā / yatanā) the householder keeps; (c) declare 'e … vratatṇā pāṁca atiyāra śodhuṁ' — 'I scrutinise the five transgressions of this vow'; (d) list those five atichāras in their Prākrit technical names; (e) close with 'e pāṁca atiyāra māṁhe' and the refrain confessing whatever transgression arose 'by day (or by night), that all of it — by mind, speech and body — micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.' Recognising this five-beat rhythm lets a reader move through the dense Prākrit lists without re-learning the frame at each vow.
keyThemes: right faith (samyaktva) as the foundation before conduct
atichāra — partial lapse versus outright breaking of a vow
niyama (firm rule) balanced by jayaṇā (prudent leeway)
the fifteen karmādāna — forbidden harmful livelihoods

glossary.definition: A partial transgression that weakens or stains a vow without fully breaking it; in this book consistently spelt 'atiyāra'. Each vow has a recognised set of five.
A vow or binding rule of conduct; the householder keeps twelve.
The five 'lesser vows' (vows 1–5): limited, layperson-scale forms of non-violence, truth, non-stealing, chastity and non-possession.
The three 'enhancing vows' (vows 6–8) that reinforce the aṇuvratas by limiting space, consumption and purposeless activity.
The four 'disciplinary/practice vows' (vows 9–12) that train the layperson toward ascetic discipline: sāmāyika, deśāvagāsika, pauṣadha, atithi-saṁvibhāga.
Right faith or right worldview — confidence in the Jina, true teacher and Jina-taught dharma; its five atichāras are reviewed before the vows themselves.
A firm personal rule or restriction the householder adopts within a vow.
Yatanā — careful, prudent leeway; the conscientious caution the layperson exercises where total avoidance is impossible (e.g. in unavoidable household activity).
Abstaining from killing/injury — the first vow (non-violence).
Abstaining from gross falsehood — the second vow (truthfulness).
Abstaining from taking what is not given — the third vow (non-stealing).
Limiting possessions — the fifth vow; the text fixes nine categories of property.
The fifteen highly harmful livelihoods (charcoal-making, forest-clearing, animal trade, etc.) that the layperson restricts under the seventh vow.
Abstaining from purposeless harm — the eighth vow, covering needless violence, careless speech and idle harmful counsel.
Sharing food and necessities with worthy ascetic guests — the twelfth vow.
'May my wrongdoing be void/undone' — the formula of confession that seals each vow's review.

howToRead: A newcomer should read it as a checklist of conscience: notice the same five-step pattern repeating for each of the twelve vows (announce the vow, name its five lapses, ask pardon), and use the glossary for the Prākrit atichāra-terms rather than trying to parse them word-by-word. An advanced practitioner will read each five-atichāra list against the canonical śrāvakācāra and observe how the Anchalgachchha recension preserves the day/night ('divasa / rātri sambandhī') substitution and the niyama-versus-jayaṇā balance.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim sūtra text, ritual placement of the short atichāra within Devasi/Rāi, and orthography (atiyāra spelling).

sources.title: Tattvārtha-sūtra of Umāsvāti, chapter 7 (vows and their atichāras)
sources.consultedFor: The doctrinal framework of the twelve vows, the five atichāras per vow, and the five atichāras of samyaktva.

sources.title: Upāsakadaśāḥ Sūtra (the ten lay-disciple narratives)
sources.consultedFor: Canonical articulation of the householder's aṇuvrata/guṇavrata/śikṣāvrata and the fifteen karmādāna.

notes: Verse-numbering caveat: the parenthetical numbers in the source — (2), (3), (4)… — are book-page markers, not vow numbers; the operative count is the Gujarati ordinals ૧–૧૨ marking the twelve vows. The five-atichāra block on samyaktva precedes vow 1 and is not itself counted among the twelve. Atichāra-term forms (e.g. 'sahasābbhakkhāṇa', 'tappaḍiruvagavavahāre') follow the book's verbatim Prākrit spelling and vary slightly from other recensions; they are preserved, not normalised. The book's spelling 'atiyāra' (with ય) is retained over the more usual 'atichāra'. URLs above are representative tradition/scholarship portals for orientation; the single golden source for the text itself is the Anchalgachchha source book. Where a specific gloss could not be independently confirmed, the canonical Tattvārtha framework was followed rather than inventing detail.
